# **Section 1 – Business Problem** {#section-1-–-business-problem}

Our client, *Lumina Data Systems* *(LDS)*, operates a large-scale global data lake that continuously ingests high-velocity streams of unstructured image data. These image assets are currently cached from LDS's structured, hierarchical databases, creating a significant intelligence gap across the enterprise. The inability to systematically search, categorise, and operationalise image data limits the business value LDS can extract from a substantial portion of its stored assets.

A key compounding challenge is the dynamic nature of LDS's data environment. New and domain-specific image sources are introduced regularly, yet existing systems cannot recognise or interpret novel inputs without costly manual intervention. This creates a scalability constraint: data volume grows continuously, but the capacity to organise and use that data does not keep pace.

*CAWAB Solutions* is addressing this challenge by developing a few-shot learning framework built on the Contrastive Image-Language Pretraining (CLIP) model architecture. Rather than requiring large, labelled training sets, which are operationally expensive to produce at LDS's scale, our approach uses a small number of labelled examples to guide classification, clustering, and similarity search across the full image data lake.

This is a data mining problem: the core task is to transform unstructured visual data into structured, searchable representations that support downstream analytical and business intelligence use cases.

# **Section 2 – Data Exploration** {#section-2-–-data-exploration}

## **2.1 \- Datasets Selected** {#2.1---datasets-selected}

We selected three benchmark datasets that provide strong diversity in the visual domain, semantic granularity, and classification difficulty: *EuroSAT, Oxford Flowers 102,* and *Stanford Cars.* EuroSAT contains 27,000 labelled satellite images from 10 land-use and land-cover classes and is derived from Sentinel-2 imagery. Oxford Flowers 102 contains 102 flower categories, with 40 to 258 images per class, and is known for substantial variation in scale, pose, and illumination. Stanford Cars contains 16,185 images from 196 classes, split into 8,144 training and 8,041 test images, making it a standard benchmark for fine-grained vehicle recognition.

![][image1]  
***Figure 1:** Comparison of class count distributions across the three selected datasets. EuroSAT exhibits the most balanced class distribution, whereas Oxford Flowers 102 exhibits the highest degree of class imbalance.*

These datasets were chosen because they highlight different aspects of visual representation learning. EuroSAT tests whether the model can separate broad scene-level categories from aerial imagery, where texture and spatial layout are often more important than object identity. In contrast, Oxford Flowers 102 and Stanford Cars are both fine-grained recognition tasks, where small visual differences between classes matter and intra-class variation can still be large. This combination provides a useful spread from relatively coarse semantic classes to highly subtle visual discrimination.

## **2.2 \- Data Audit and Preprocessing** {#2.2---data-audit-and-preprocessing}

Before running the few-shot experiments, a data audit was conducted to ensure that all selected datasets, including EuroSAT, Oxford Flowers 102, and Stanford Cars, were loaded correctly and matched the intended evaluation protocol. This included verifying that images could be read without corruption, that class labels were mapped consistently, and that all classes were available for sampling under the *few-shot\_all\_classes* protocol. Since the first protocol requires support examples from every class, it was important to confirm that each dataset contained sufficient valid samples per class for the selected shot settings of 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 shots.

All experiments were built around *OpenCLIP ViT-B-32-256* with pretrained weights *datacomp\_s34b\_b86k* from HuggingFace. To maintain consistency with the pretrained model, image preprocessing followed the standard OpenCLIP train and evaluation transforms. This ensures that the input distribution seen during fine-tuning and evaluation remains aligned with the model’s original training setup, reducing unnecessary mismatch between benchmark data and the pretrained visual encoder.

For training-time preprocessing, the OpenCLIP transforms were applied to the support images used in few-shot adaptation. For evaluation, the corresponding OpenCLIP evaluation transforms were used on test images to provide a deterministic and standardised inference pipeline. This preprocessing step is especially important because the three selected datasets differ substantially in the visual domain: EuroSAT contains aerial remote-sensing imagery, Oxford Flowers 102 contains natural fine-grained flower photos, and Stanford Cars contains fine-grained vehicle images. Using a shared model-specific preprocessing pipeline helps ensure that performance differences are driven more by dataset difficulty than by inconsistent image handling.

The few-shot setup was evaluated across five shot levels (1, 2, 4, 8, and 16\) and three random seeds (1, 2, and 3\) to reduce sensitivity to a single support set sample. The primary evaluation metric was test top-1 accuracy, which measures the proportion of correctly classified test examples. A secondary metric, macro accuracy, was also measured to better capture per-class performance, especially in settings where class imbalance may affect the interpretation of standard accuracy. Together, these choices provide a more reliable view of model behaviour across coarse-grained and fine-grained datasets under low-data conditions.

## **2.3 \- Label Distribution** {#2.3---label-distribution}

The degree of label granularity and class balance varies notably in the selected datasets.   
EuroSAT is the largest dataset with the most samples, despite having the fewest classes, while the opposite is true for the Oxford Flowers 102 dataset.  
**![][image2]**  
***Figure 2:** Comparison of dataset scale in terms of total images and number of classes. EuroSAT has the largest image count but the smallest number of classes, whereas Stanford Cars has the largest number of classes. Oxford Flowers 102 has the smallest image count of the three datasets.*

*EuroSAT* is comparatively balanced, with 27,000 samples over 10 classes, giving a much more even class structure than the two fine-grained datasets.  
![][image3]  
***Figure 3:** Composition of the EuroSAT train, validation, and test splits for the few-shot all-classes protocol. The split consists of 16,200 training images (60%), 5,400 validation images (20%), and 5,400 test images (20%).*

*Oxford Flowers 102* is more imbalanced: each category contains between 40 and 258 images, so some flower classes are represented far more heavily than others. This uneven distribution can bias training towards better-represented classes and make minority classes harder to learn.  
![][image4]  
***Figure 4:** Composition of the Flowers102 train, validation, and test splits for the few-shot all-classes protocol. The split consists of 5,242 training images (71.1%), 818 validation images (11.1%), and 1,310 test images (17.8%).*

*Stanford Cars* contains 196 classes with a roughly even train/test split per class, but it still remains challenging because the number of classes is large relative to the number of examples per class. In practice, this means the task is not dominated by severe class imbalance but by the difficulty of distinguishing visually similar car makes, models, and years.  
![][image5]  
***Figure 5:** Stanford Cars protocol split showing the composition of the train pool, validation set, and test set under the few-shot all-classes setting. The split consists of 6,547 training images (80.4%), 809 validation images (9.9%), and 788 test images (9.7%).*

## **2.4 \- Image Properties** {#2.4---image-properties}

The three datasets also differ strongly in image characteristics. *EuroSAT* consists of satellite patches derived from Sentinel-2 imagery, so its visual patterns are dominated by land texture, geometry, vegetation, water, and built environments rather than foreground objects. Sentinel-2 itself is a multispectral Earth observation mission designed for land monitoring, and EuroSAT images have that remote-sensing viewpoint.

*Oxford Flowers 102* contains natural photographs of flowers with substantial variation in scale, pose, and lighting, as well as visually similar categories and large within-class variation. These properties make the dataset challenging for fine-grained classification because the model must focus on subtle differences in shape, petal, and colour cues while remaining robust to background clutter and viewpoint shifts.

*Stanford Cars* contains images of cars from 196 fine-grained categories, primarily distinguished by make, model, and year. Most images contain a single prominent car, but the differences between categories are often visually subtle. This means that small features such as headlight shape, grille design, body contour, and viewpoint become especially important.

From a modelling perspective, these image properties are useful because they span three distinct regimes: scene-level remote sensing, biologically fine-grained natural images, and fine-grained manufactured objects. Strong performance across all three is therefore a stronger indicator of representation quality than success on only one domain.

## **2.5 \- Embedding Analysis (t-SNE)** {#2.5---embedding-analysis-(t-sne)}

To qualitatively assess the representation quality of the image embeddings, we visualised the learned feature space using t-SNE for *EuroSAT*, *Oxford Flowers 102*, and *Stanford Cars*. These plots provide an intuitive view of how well samples from the same class group together and how clearly different classes are separated in the embedding space.

Overall, the three datasets show noticeably different clustering behaviour, which aligns with their task difficulty and class granularity.

![][image6]  
***Figure 6:** t-SNE visualisation of EuroSAT image embeddings extracted with OpenCLIP, with points coloured by class. The projection shows several locally coherent class clusters, indicating that semantically similar land-use categories are partially separated in the embedding space.*

The *EuroSAT* t-SNE plot shows the clearest large-scale structure among the three datasets. Several classes form relatively distinct regions, with samples arranged in separate clusters in the embedding space. In particular, there is a visible coarse separation between land-cover categories, suggesting that the embeddings capture broad scene-level semantics effectively.

However, some overlap remains in the central region of the plot, where visually related classes appear closer together. This is expected as certain satellite categories share similar textures and spatial patterns, making the decision boundaries less clean than in simpler classification settings.

![][image7]  
***Figure 7:** t-SNE projection of Flowers102 OpenCLIP image embeddings, coloured by class. The visualisation shows a larger number of fine-grained classes with several compact local clusters, although substantial overlap remains between visually similar flower categories.*

The *Oxford Flowers 102* plot shows a more fragmented but still meaningful clustering pattern. Many classes form small, compact local groups, which suggests that the embeddings are capable of capturing fine-grained visual cues. However, these groups are distributed densely across the space, and some classes lie close to one another, reflecting the high visual similarity between flower species.

This pattern indicates that the representation space contains useful class-specific information, but the separation margin between classes is smaller than in EuroSAT. The model appears to learn fine-grained distinctions, yet the task remains challenging because subtle differences in petal shape, colour, and structure can produce neighbouring or partially overlapping clusters.

![][image8]  
***Figure 8:** t-SNE visualisation of Stanford Cars image embeddings extracted with OpenCLIP, with points coloured by class. The embedding space exhibits considerable inter-class mixing, indicating that many fine-grained car categories remain difficult to distinguish using the pretrained representation alone.*

The *Stanford Cars* t-SNE plot appears the most mixed and diffuse of the three. While there are some small local clusters, the embedding space is much more entangled overall, with many classes scattered throughout overlapping regions. This suggests that the model has greater difficulty learning strongly separated representations for the car categories.

This result can be explained by the nature of the dataset. Stanford Cars is a highly fine-grained benchmark where many categories differ only by minor changes in model year, trim, body shape, or front-end design. These subtle differences make inter-class separation much harder, especially when viewpoint, lighting, and background variation are also present. The weaker class separation in the t-SNE plot, therefore, reflects the intrinsic difficulty of the task rather than a failure of the embedding method alone.

## **2.6 \- Data Limitations and Contamination Risk** {#2.6---data-limitations-and-contamination-risk}

Despite being widely used benchmarks, these datasets have several limitations. First, all three are relatively curated academic datasets and may not reflect the full diversity or messiness of real-world deployment data. This means strong benchmark performance may overestimate practical robustness.

Second, Oxford Flowers 102 and Stanford Cars are both fine-grained datasets with limited examples per class relative to task difficulty, which increases sensitivity to spurious background cues, viewpoint bias, and overfitting to class-specific visual artefacts. Oxford Flowers 102 also has explicit class imbalance, with some categories containing far more images than others.

Third, *EuroSAT* is derived from Sentinel-2 imagery and therefore reflects a specific sensing pipeline and geographic sampling process. Models performing well on EuroSAT may still face domain shifts under different sensors, resolutions, seasons, or regions.

A further concern is contamination risk, especially when evaluating pretrained vision-language models. Since *EuroSAT, Oxford Flowers 102*, and *Stanford Cars* are all well-known public benchmarks, there is a non-trivial possibility that some images, metadata, or closely related examples were seen during large-scale pretraining data collection. This can inflate downstream performance and make it harder to separate true generalisation from benchmark familiarity. The contamination risk is especially relevant for foundation models trained on broad internet-scale corpora, where dataset provenance is often only partially transparent.

To mitigate this risk, results from the implementations should be interpreted with more caution. Performance on these benchmarks is still useful for relative comparison between methods, but it should not be treated as definitive evidence of out-of-distribution robustness or real-world transfer.

# **Section 3 – Initial Findings** {#section-3-–-initial-findings}

This section presents preliminary results from our method reproduction experiments. There are three implementations of ‘few-shot adaptation’ techniques, and they are evaluated against the zero-shot CLIP baseline on at least one benchmark dataset. Full comparative results will be consolidated in the final report.

**Disclaimer: Due to computational constraints, only PromptSRC was reproduced in full and tested, other methods were reproduced in a superficial sense. We elected to divert the compute budget to experimenting and iterating on the proposed method, as this involves a longer self-supervised stage after few-shot mid-training inspired by PromptKD. Further, we select PromptSRC to reproduce as our method builds on top of PromptSRC; thus, accurate quantification of performance uplift with the second post-training stage is critical.** 

## **3.1 \- Zero-Shot CLIP Baseline** {#3.1---zero-shot-clip-baseline}

Zero-shot CLIP is the baseline used to measure how well the frozen CLIP model performs before any few-shot adaptation. The core idea is straightforward: instead of training prompts, adapters, projection heads, or classifier weights, we use CLIP exactly as it was pretrained. No model weights are updated, and no labelled training images are used for fitting.

**Set-up**

For the backbone, we used the shared OpenCLIP ViT-B-32-256 model pretrained on datacomp\_s34b\_b86k. This is the same base model used by the other methods in the repo, which makes the zero-shot baseline a fair control row. The only dataset-specific information given to the model is the class names and a small set of hand-written text templates.

For each class, the baseline builds text prompts using dataset-specific templates. For EuroSAT, the prompts describe satellite photos; for Flowers102, they describe flower images; and for Stanford Cars, they describe car models. Each prompt is encoded by the frozen CLIP text encoder, and the template features for each class are averaged into one normalised text feature. These class text features form the zero-shot classifier.

During evaluation, each image is passed through the frozen CLIP image encoder to produce a normalised image feature. Classification is then done by comparing the image feature with every class text feature using CLIP’s scaled similarity score. The predicted class is simply the class with the highest similarity. There is no training loop, no loss function, no epochs, and no learned parameters.

**What We Observed**

Zero-shot CLIP performed strongly without using any labelled training images for fitting. On EuroSAT, it achieved a test top-1 accuracy of 59.19%. On Flowers102, it reached 77.40%, and on Stanford Cars, it reached 89.97%. These results demonstrate that the pre-trained OpenCLIP model already possesses useful visual-language knowledge before any few-shot adaptation.

![][image9]  
***Figure 9:** Zero-shot CLIP top-1 accuracy on the test split across the three selected datasets. Stanford Cars achieves the highest accuracy (90.0%), followed by Flowers102 (77.4%) and EuroSAT (59.2%)*

The performance varied by dataset. Stanford Cars was the strongest zero-shot result, suggesting that the pretrained CLIP representation and car text prompts align very well with this dataset. Flowers102 was also strong at about 77%, while EuroSAT was lower at approximately 59%, likely because satellite-image classes are more visually specialised and less like the natural-image data CLIP typically learns from.

## **3.2 \- PromptSRC** {#3.2---promptsrc}

PromptSRC approaches few-shot CLIP adaptation through prompt learning and self-regularisation. The core idea is that we do not fine-tune CLIP itself. Instead, we keep the OpenCLIP image and text encoders frozen and train only a very small set of learnable text-prompt parameters. These prompts adapt the classifier to the target dataset while the regularisation terms stop the model from drifting too far away from CLIP’s original zero-shot behaviour.

**![][image10]**

***Figure 10:** Overview of the PromptSRC architecture. PromptSRC extends CLIP by introducing learnable visual and textual prompts while keeping the pretrained image and text encoders frozen. Training is guided by classification loss and self-regularisation objectives that promote consistency between prompted and frozen representations, improving adaptation in few-shot settings.*

**Set-up**

For the backbone, we used OpenCLIP ViT-B-32-256 pretrained on datacomp\_s34b\_b86k, which is the shared model used across the project. PromptSRC builds a frozen zero-shot reference by encoding dataset-specific text templates, such as satellite-photo templates for EuroSAT and flower-specific templates for Flowers102. These fixed text features act as an anchor during training: the learned prompts are allowed to improve few-shot classification, but they are encouraged to preserve the useful generalisation already present in CLIP.

The trainable part of the model is a CoOp-style text prompt learner. It learns four context tokens initialised from the phrase *“a photo of a"* and then attaches the class name after those learned tokens. For example, instead of using only a fixed hand-written prompt, the model learns a small prompt prefix that can better fit the dataset. The image encoder remains completely frozen, and the text transformer also remains frozen; only the context-token tensor is updated. In this OpenCLIP port, visual prompting is disabled because the official PromptSRC visual prompts require modified CLIP internals. As a result, the implemented method is a text-prompt version of PromptSRC with self-regularisation and Gaussian prompt aggregation.

The number of trainable parameters is extremely small. With four learned context tokens and a 512-dimensional text embedding space, only about 2,048 parameters are trained. This is negligible compared with the full CLIP model and is far below even 0.01% of the model’s total parameters. Everything else, including the image tower, text tower, token embeddings, and logit scale, stays frozen.

Training used the shared few-shot protocol across EuroSAT, Flowers102, and Stanford Cars with 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 labelled examples per class, evaluated over seeds 1, 2, and 3\. The model was trained for 50 epochs using SGD with a learning rate of 0.0025 and batch size 4\. The loss combines standard cross-entropy with three PromptSRC-style regularisation terms: a text-feature regularisation loss against the frozen zero-shot text features, an image-feature regularisation loss, and a KL-divergence loss that encourages the learned-prompt logits to stay close to the frozen zero-shot logits. Gaussian prompt aggregation is then used to combine prompt states across training, rather than relying only on the final epoch.

At inference time, classification is done using the frozen image encoder and the learned text prompts. Images are encoded into CLIP image features, the learned prompts are encoded into text features, and the final prediction comes from the scaled similarity between image and text features. No model weights are updated during inference, and no extra classifier or projection head is needed.

**What We Observed**

PromptSRC improved steadily as the number of shots increased. Averaged across all three datasets and three seeds, test top-1 accuracy rose from about 78.85% in the 1-shot setting to about 85.92% in the 16-shot setting. EuroSAT showed the largest improvement, increasing from about 64.45% at 1 shot to 78.41% at 16 shots. Flowers102 also improved clearly, moving from about 82.04% to 88.22%. Stanford Cars started very strong at about 90.06% in the 1-shot setting and reached about 91.12% at 16 shots, suggesting that the frozen CLIP representation was already highly effective for that dataset.

![][image11]

***Figure 11:** PromptSRC test top-1 accuracy across different numbers of labelled examples per class, averaged over three seeds. Performance improves as the number of shots increases for EuroSAT and Flowers102, while Stanford Cars remains consistently strong with only marginal gains from additional supervision.*

Overall, the results are promising because PromptSRC achieves meaningful adaptation while training only a few thousand parameters. The method is especially useful in the few-shot setting because it gives the model enough flexibility to adjust to a dataset without sacrificing the generalisation strength of the original frozen CLIP model.

## **3.3 \- DPC** {#3.3---dpc}

DPC approaches a few-shot CLIP adaptation as a plug-in on top of a prompt-tuned backbone. The core idea is straightforward: first train a normal prompt-learning backbone, then add a second parallel prompt that focuses specifically on the classes the backbone finds confusing. The CLIP encoders stay frozen throughout; only small text-prompt parameters are trained.

![][image12]

***Figure 12:** Overview of the DPC architecture. DPC improves prompt tuning by introducing dual-prompt collaboration: a tuned prompt for adaptation and a parallel prompt for preserving transferable base knowledge. During training, hard negative samples and contrastive learning are used to strengthen discrimination, while inference combines the two prompts through weighted aggregation to balance base and novel class generalisation.*

**Set-up**

In this repository, DPC, with the same backbone as other methods in this repository, is implemented as an extension of a PromptSRC-style learned text prompt, rather than the full official DPC setup. This design keeps the method compatible with the shared few-shot pipeline, common data splits, and consistent evaluation metrics.

The model uses two learned text prompts. The first is the backbone prompt, trained in the first stage. It follows the same idea as the PromptSRC text prompt: four learnable context tokens are inserted before each class name, starting from the phrase *“a photo of a”*. This prompt learns a dataset-specific classifier while remaining close to CLIP’s original zero-shot behaviour.

The second is the DPC prompt. After training the backbone prompt, its learned context tokens are copied to initialise the DPC prompt. The backbone prompt is then frozen, and only the DPC prompt is updated. The role of this second prompt is not simply to increase the score of the correct class but to better handle hard negatives, classes that the backbone already considers plausible but that are still incorrect. In this sense, DPC aims to sharpen the decision boundary around the most confusing alternatives.

All other model components remain frozen throughout training. The OpenCLIP image encoder, text transformer, token embeddings, and zero-shot reference features are not updated. Each prompt contains four 512-dimensional context tokens, giving roughly 2,048 trainable parameters per prompt. Because the model stores both the backbone prompt and the DPC prompt, the full dual-prompt setup has about 4,096 trainable prompt parameters, which is still far below 0.01% of the full CLIP model.

Training is carried out in two stages. In the first stage, the backbone prompt is trained for 20 epochs using a combination of cross-entropy loss and a retention loss, which keeps its logits close to those of the frozen zero-shot CLIP model. In the second stage, the DPC prompt is trained for another 20 epochs. Its objective combines standard cross-entropy, a hard-negative loss, a retention loss against the frozen backbone prompt, and a prompt-proximity loss that prevents the DPC text features from drifting too far from the backbone text features.

At inference time, the two prompts are used together. The model computes one set of logits from the backbone prompt and another from the DPC prompt, then combines them through a weighted average. In the current configuration, the backbone prompt contributes 80% of the final score, and the DPC prompt contributes 20%. As a result, the classifier remains primarily guided by the more stable backbone prompt, while the DPC prompt provides a smaller corrective signal for difficult class confusions.

**What We Observed**

Due to computational constraints, full quantitative results for DPC across all shot levels and datasets were not completed for this mid-project update. The dual-prompt architecture was successfully implemented and validated on a small subset of *EuroSAT*. Preliminary observations suggest that the second DPC prompt provides a modest corrective signal over the backbone prompt alone, consistent with the behaviour reported in the original paper. Full results across all three datasets and shot levels will be included in the final report.

## **3.4 \- PromptKD** {#3.4---promptkd}

PromptKD approaches few-shot CLIP adaptation through knowledge distillation. The core idea is straightforward: rather than updating the model's weights directly, we train a small set of additional parameters that help a lighter student model learn from a stronger teacher. Both models stay frozen throughout; only the extra components are trained.

![][image13]

***Figure 13:** Overview of the PromptKD architecture. PromptKD first trains a large teacher prompt model on labelled data, then distils its knowledge into a smaller student model using unlabelled domain images. During inference, only the lightweight student model is used, enabling efficient few-shot adaptation with reduced computational cost.*

**Setup**

For the teacher, we used *ConvNeXt-Base* pretrained on *LAION-Aesthetics*, and for the student, we used *ViT-B-32-256* pretrained on DataComp. Both were loaded through OpenCLIP. The teacher is the larger, more capable model; its job is to generate rich, soft predictions that guide the student during training.

The student has two small trainable components attached to it. The first is a visual prompt, which is a learnable tensor the same size as the input image that gets added to every image before it enters the encoder. Interpret it as a learned filter that nudges the image in a direction that helps classification. The second is a projection head, a single linear layer that maps the student's 512-dimensional features into the teacher's 640-dimensional space so the two can be directly compared during distillation.

Everything else, both encoders and both text towers, stays completely frozen. In total, only around 606,000 parameters are trained, which is less than 0.1% of the full model.

Training used 16 labelled images per class from the Flowers102 dataset, run for 5 epochs. The loss combines standard cross-entropy against the ground truth labels with a KL divergence term that pushes the student's output distribution to match the teacher's. In inference, the projection head is discarded, and classification is done directly from the student's raw features.

 

| Method | Accuracy |
| :---- | :---- |
| Zero-shot CLIP (ConvNeXt-Base) | 72.91% |
| PromptKD – 16 shots, 5 epochs | 74.86% |
| Improvement | \+1.95% |

***Table 1:** Accuracy comparison between Zero-shot and PromptKD model*

**What We Observed**

PromptKD improved over the zero-shot baseline by about 2 percentage points using only 16 examples per class. Training loss dropped consistently across all five epochs, which confirmed the model was genuinely learning rather than fluctuating. The result is encouraging, given how little data and how few trainable parameters were involved; it suggests the teacher's richer representations are successfully transferring into the smaller student through the distillation loss.

## **3.5 \- Comparative Summary** {#3.5---comparative-summary}

Based on our reproduction results between Zero-Shot CLIP and PromptSRC across all three datasets, PromptSRC improves on zero-shot CLIP, but the size of the gain depends strongly on the dataset. The largest improvement is seen on EuroSAT, where zero-shot accuracy starts at 59.2%, and PromptSRC rises from 64.5% at 1-shot to 78.4% at 16-shot. This shows that EuroSAT benefits the most from few-shot prompt learning, suggesting that the pretrained zero-shot classifier is less well aligned with the remote-sensing domain but can adapt effectively once a small amount of labelled data is introduced.

A moderate improvement is observed on Oxford Flowers 102\. Zero-shot CLIP already performs reasonably well at 77.4%, and PromptSRC increases this to 82.0% at 1-shot and 88.2% at 16-shot. This indicates that the pretrained model already captures useful semantic structure for flower recognition, while prompt tuning further sharpens class discrimination under few-shot supervision.

In contrast, the performance on the Stanford Cars dataset shows only a small gain. Zero-shot CLIP is already very strong at 90.0%, and PromptSRC improves this only slightly to 90.1% at 1-shot and 91.1% at 16-shot. This suggests that the pretrained OpenCLIP backbone already provides highly effective features for this dataset, leaving relatively limited room for improvement through prompt adaptation.

Overall, the results show a clear trend: the lower the zero-shot baseline, the larger the benefit from PromptSRC. We believe that prompt tuning delivers greater value when the pretrained model is less naturally aligned with the target domain, while datasets that are already well generalised by zero-shot CLIP see smaller incremental performance gains.

## **3.6 \- Proposed Novel Method** {#3.6---proposed-novel-method}

PromptKD presents an insight valuable to investigate further, that few-shot training provides enough conditioning for unsupervised training to provide useful learning signals on the domain of the few-shot examples.

Thus inspired by results from promptSRC and promptKD, we propose a two phase few-shot learning regime involving a mid-training stage with few-shot examples based on promptSRC, and a second unsupervised post-training stage.

A core issue we find however is that PromptKD's teacher, which provides the performance uplift from few-shot learning, is frozen. While distillation via KL divergence minimisation is an effective strategy, the target model is ultimately trained purely on the few-shot examples. Thus we reason that the student model’s potential capability improvement is upper bounded not by the unsupervised training distribution, but rather the teacher’s few-shot learning. 

In other words, the post-training stage is missing key learning value as the learning objective is tied to the previous training stage.

We replace the frozen-teacher to student distillation objective of the post-training phase with a PAWs (Assran et al., 2021\) objective (fig. 14). Where we use the few-shot trained model to embed images across an anchor and augmented view of an image in the unlabelled dataset, assign pseudo-labels using promptSRC’s classification method, and use a similarity maximisation task between the label distributions of the anchor and augmented view. 

This can be thought of intuitively as similar to a contrastive learning objective, where instead of operating over the cosine similarity between image embeddings, we operate over the pseudo-label distribution, which are assigned based on the provided few-shot examples, allowing us to simultaneously use the few-shot training and examples during post-training, without necessarily freezing the model to a fixed target, as embeddings and label assignment can change with encoder updates. 

We justify that this method is few-shot, as it still only requires a limited number of labelled samples, however concede that this method is not true “few-shot”  in that it requires a wider, unlabelled dataset. We justify this as the large cost of data and ML project costs lie not in data collection but rather the cost of high quality labelling.   
![][image14]  
***Figure 14:** PAWs training framework (Assran et al, 2021\)*	

# **Section 4 – Difficulties Encountered** {#section-4-–-difficulties-encountered}

## **4.1 \- Data Difficulties** {#4.1---data-difficulties}

The following data-related difficulties were encountered during the exploration phase:

* Potential benchmark data contamination in CLIP pre-training: Several standard evaluation datasets (including Flowers102 and EuroSAT) are likely included in CLIP’s pre-training corpus, meaning zero-shot and few-shot accuracy figures may be inflated relative to a truly unseen deployment scenario. This was identified early during the data audit phase. The team agreed to reframe all results as improvements over the zero-shot CLIP baseline rather than as absolute accuracy claims, which preserves the comparative validity of our findings for the client.

* Class imbalance and limited few-shot sample diversity: In the 16-shot setting, some classes had limited visual variation among the labelled examples (e.g., highly similar flower images within a single category in Flowers102). This constrained the richness of prototype representations in DPC and reduced the quality of the distillation signal in PromptKD. The team mitigated this by using stratified sampling during few-shot set construction, ensuring examples were drawn from diverse image conditions where available.

![][image15]  
***Figure 15:** Number of supervised training images used in the few-shot setting across datasets and shot counts. Although EuroSAT contains the largest number of total images overall, it has the smallest few-shot supervised train set at each shot level because it has the fewest classes.*

* The data pipeline is still fragile: It relies on downloading data from external sources and does not yet robustly handle the inconsistent formats and structures across datasets, which slowed experimentation and made the overall workflow less reliable. To address this, the team is refactoring the data loading scripts to include format validation checks and local caching, which will reduce reliance on external downloads and improve pipeline stability for the remaining experimental runs.

## **4.2 \- Compute Constraint** {#4.2---compute-constraint}

Reproducing multiple methods required careful allocation of limited computational resources. Since the available compute had to be shared across all three planned reproductions, we were not able to perform exhaustive hyperparameter sweeps or a large number of repeated trials for each method. Instead, resources had to be managed conservatively to ensure that sufficient budget remained for the later stages of the project. This is exacerbated by the fact that our method requires training on a wider unlabelled dataset on top of standard few-shot learning.

As a result, under the available compute constraints, we were only able to fully establish the zero-shot CLIP baseline and the few-shot PromptSRC reproduction. The remaining compute budget has been reserved for training and evaluating our proposed method.

## **4.3 \- Risk Register** {#4.3---risk-register}

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Data contamination (benchmark overlap with CLIP pre-training) | Medium | High | Reframe all results as improvements over the zero-shot CLIP baseline rather than absolute accuracy claims; this approach is already implemented across all three method reproductions. For the novel contribution phase, evaluate at least one dataset less commonly included in CLIP pre-training data (e.g. domain-specific remote sensing or medical imaging benchmarks). |
| Dataset scale and compute constraints limiting experiment scope | High | Medium | Use smaller dataset subsets (e.g. 10-class subsets of EuroSAT or Flowers102) for initial development and hyperparameter tuning runs, then scale up to full datasets for final reported results. Leverage Google Colab Pro GPU resources and stagger compute-heavy runs to avoid contention across team members. |
| Method reproduction failure or significant deviation from published results | Low | High | Reference official code repositories and published supplementary materials before implementing from scratch. Document all implementation decisions and deviations from the paper in version-controlled code comments. If results cannot be reproduced within 10% of published accuracy, fall back to the zero-shot baseline, document the deviation transparently in the final report, and redirect effort towards the novel contribution phase. |
| Team scheduling conflicts or uneven workload distribution | Medium | Low | Internal deadlines with a 3-day buffer built in before the formal submission date; all work tracked via a shared GitHub repository with pull request reviews required before merging. Weekly check-ins are scheduled every Monday with the supervisor for any questions. |
| Scope creep in the novel contribution phase | Low | High | Agree on a fixed novel contribution direction by Week 8 via team vote; any extension requests require consensus approval and must be assessed against the final deadline. Maintain a ‘stretch goals’ list so low-priority ideas are captured but not activated without explicit sign-off. |

***Table 2:** Risk register summarising identified project risks, likelihood, impact, and mitigation strategies*

# **Section 5 – Updated Project Plan** {#section-5-–-updated-project-plan}

Compared to the plan submitted in Assignment 1, the overall scope and CRISP-DM methodology remain unchanged. The main deviation is that dataset preparation and zero-shot baseline evaluation took approximately one week longer than originally anticipated (moved to Week 5\) due to the compute environment setup and the revised evaluation strategy adopted following the data contamination discussion. As a result, the method reproduction phase was shifted one week later (Weeks 6-7 inc. StuVac). All three method reproductions are now either complete or substantially underway, and the project remains on track to meet the final submission deadline.

The novel contribution currently in development is an extension of PromptSRC that incorporates a self-supervised post-training stage inspired by PromptKD, aiming to improve few-shot generalisation beyond what prompt tuning alone achieves.

| Task | Owner | Timeline | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Literature review and baseline CLIP setup | All | Weeks 1-3 | Complete |
| Dataset preparation | Antoine | Weeks 3-4\* | Complete |
| Data exploration & audit | Antoine | Weeks 4-5\* | Complete |
| Zero-shot CLIP baseline evaluation | Antoine | Weeks 4-5\* | Complete |
| Method reproduction – PromptKD | Aryan | Weeks 5-6\* | Complete |
| Method reproduction – PromptSRC | Wendy | Weeks 5-6\* | Complete |
| Method reproduction – DPC | Coral | Weeks 5-6\* | Complete |
| Results comparison, difficulties & plan update   | Benjamin | Week 7\* | Complete |
| Novel method/improvement development | All | Weeks 8-11 | In progress |
| Client final report drafting | All | Weeks 10-11 | Planned |
| Internal academic report | All | Week 12 | Planned |
| Final presentation | All | Week 12 | Planned |

***Table 3:** Updated project plan with task owners, timelines, and current status. Note: An asterisk (\*) means the task was delayed by a week.*

Key milestone dates (changed from Assignment 1):

* Mid-Project Update (Assignment 2): 15 April 2026  
* Client Final Report (External): 17 May 2026  
* Team Final Report and Presentation (Internal): 17 May 2026


# **Section 6 – Additional Considerations** {#section-6-–-additional-considerations}

There are two additional matters the client should be aware of as the project moves into its final phase:

* **Responsible use and deployment considerations:** The few-shot classification system developed in this project will ultimately be applied to real-world image assets within LDS’s data lake. Before deployment, any classification outputs must be treated as decision-support signals rather than definitive labels, particularly in high-stakes business contexts. CAWAB Solutions recommends that LDS establish a human-in-the-loop review process for edge cases where the model’s confidence score falls below a defined threshold. This is especially relevant given the contamination risk discussed in Section 2.6, which means that benchmark accuracy figures may not fully reflect real-world deployment performance.

* **Scalability of the proposed approach:** The current framework uses OpenCLIP ViT-B-32-256 as its backbone, which is lightweight enough for efficient inference at scale. The few-shot prompt-tuning approach is particularly well-suited to LDS’s operational context because new image categories can be registered with as few as one to sixteen labelled examples per class, without retraining the underlying model. This significantly reduces the labelling burden compared with traditional supervised approaches and supports the continuous onboarding of novel image sources that LDS encounters in its data lake operations. The final report will include a more detailed discussion of inference latency and throughput estimates relevant to LDS’s production environment.


  # **References** {#references}

* Assran, M., Caron, M., Misra, I., Bojanowski, P., Joulin, A., Ballas, N., & Rabbat, M. (2021). Semi-supervised learning of visual features by non-parametrically predicting view assignments with support samples. *Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)*, 8443–8452. [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2104.13963](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2104.13963)

* Huang, Y., Shakeri, F., Dolz, J., Boudiaf, M., Bahig, H., & Ben, A. I. (2024). *LP++: A Surprisingly Strong Linear Probe for Few-Shot CLIP*. ArXiv.org. [https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.02285](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.02285)   
* Helber, P., Bischke, B., Dengel, A., & Borth, D. (2019). EuroSAT: A novel dataset and deep learning benchmark for land use and land cover classification. *IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing*, *12*(7), 2217–2226. [https://doi.org/10.1109/JSTARS.2019.2918242](https://doi.org/10.1109/JSTARS.2019.2918242)

* Khattak, M. U., Wasim, S. T., Naseer, M., Khan, S., Yang, M.-H., & Khan, F. S. (2023b). *Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting*. ArXiv.org. *Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)*, 15190–15200. [https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06948](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06948)  
* Krause, J., Stark, M., Deng, J., & Fei-Fei, L. (2013). 3D object representations for fine-grained categorization. *4th IEEE Workshop on 3D Representation and Recognition (3dRR-13), ICCV Workshops*, 554–561. [https://www.cs.princeton.edu/\~jiadeng/paper/KrauseStarkDengFei-Fei\_3DRR13.pdf](https://www.cs.princeton.edu/~jiadeng/paper/KrauseStarkDengFei-Fei_3DRR13.pdf)   
* Lafon, M., Ramzi, E., Rambour, C., Audebert, N., & Thome, N. (2024). *GalLoP: Learning Global and Local Prompts for Vision-Language Models*. ArXiv.org. [https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.01400](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.01400)   
* Li, H., Wang, L., Wang, C., Jiang, J., Peng, Y., & Long, G. (2025). *DPC: Dual-Prompt Collaboration for Tuning Vision-Language Models*. ArXiv.org. [https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13443](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13443)

PromptKD: Unsupervised prompt distillation for vision-language models. *Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)*, 26617–26626. [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2403.02781](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2403.02781)

* Yu, T., Lu, Z., Jin, X., Chen, Z., & Wang, X. (2022). *Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models*. ArXiv.org. [https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.10277](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.10277)

* Zhang, C., Lu, R., Zeeshan Hayder, & Li, H. (2025). *DPD-LoRA: Dynamic Prompt-Driven Low-Rank Adaptation for Improved Generalization*. Openreview.net. [https://openreview.net/forum?id=Dci14asFPV](https://openreview.net/forum?id=Dci14asFPV)   
* Zhang, R., Zhang, W., Fang, R., Gao, Li, K., Dai, J., Qiao, Y., & Li, H. (2022). Tip-Adapter: Training-free Adaption of CLIP for Few-shot Classification. *ArXiv (Cornell University)*. [https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2207.09519](https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2207.09519)
